questions.question: Explain the role of operation select lines in ALU. How many select lines are needed for selecting between 16 different operations?
keyPoints: Operation select lines are control signals from control unit that specify which operation ALU performs
They connect to multiplexers that choose output from appropriate functional unit
For 2^n operations, n select lines are required
For 16 operations (2^4), 4 select lines are needed

questions.question: Describe the four status flags maintained by ALU after arithmetic and logical operations. Under what conditions is each flag set?
keyPoints: Zero flag: Set when result equals zero
Carry flag: Set when unsigned overflow occurs (carry out or borrow)
Sign flag: Set when MSB of result is 1 (negative in two's complement)
Overflow flag: Set when signed overflow occurs (result sign incorrect)

questions.question: Explain how the ALU performs subtraction using two's complement representation.
keyPoints: Subtraction A - B is performed as A + (NOT B) + 1
The subtrahend B is inverted (bitwise NOT)
1 is added to the inverted value using the adder circuit
This allows subtraction using the same adder hardware as addition

questions.question: What is the difference between arithmetic shift and logical shift operations in ALU?
keyPoints: Logical shift fills vacated bits with zeros, treats number as unsigned
Arithmetic right shift preserves the sign bit (MSB)
Arithmetic shift suitable for signed division by powers of 2
Logical shift suitable for unsigned operations and bit manipulation

questions.question: Explain the purpose of multiplexer (MUX) in ALU design with the help of a block diagram.
keyPoints: MUX selects between outputs of different functional units (adder, logic unit, shifter)
Selection is controlled by operation select lines
Allows single ALU to perform multiple operations
Output of MUX becomes the ALU result based on selected operation

questions.question: What is bypass logic in ALU data paths? When is it used and what advantage does it provide?
keyPoints: Bypass logic allows data to circumvent the ALU when no computation needed
Used for register-to-register data transfer operations
Saves unnecessary ALU processing cycles
Improves instruction throughput for simple data movement

questions.question: Explain how logical operations AND and OR are used for bit manipulation in ALU.
keyPoints: AND with mask clears (sets to 0) specified bit positions
OR with mask sets (sets to 1) specified bit positions
XOR with mask toggles (inverts) specified bit positions
Mask has 1s in positions to affect, 0s in positions to preserve

questions.question: Draw and explain the organization of a typical ALU showing arithmetic circuit, logic circuit, and shifter. Explain how these units are integrated using multiplexer selection.
keyPoints: Arithmetic circuit handles addition, subtraction using full adders
Logic circuit handles AND, OR, NOT, XOR operations bitwise
Shifter performs logical and arithmetic shifts
MUX selects output based on operation select lines
Control unit generates select signals from instruction opcode
Result and status flags are produced for each operation

questions.question: Explain the concept of signed overflow in ALU operations. How does it differ from unsigned overflow (carry)? Provide examples to illustrate when each type occurs.
keyPoints: Signed overflow occurs in signed arithmetic when result exceeds representable range
It occurs when adding two same-sign numbers produces opposite-sign result
Overflow flag indicates this condition for signed operations
Carry flag indicates unsigned overflow (carry out)
Overflow = math error in signed interpretation, Carry = unsigned range error
Example: 4-bit: 0111 + 0100 = 1011 (-5), overflow since 7+4=11 cannot be represented

questions.question: Describe the complete process of executing an arithmetic instruction (e.g., ADD R1, R2, R3) including the role of ALU, registers, and control signals.
keyPoints: Instruction fetch: Load instruction from memory to IR
Instruction decode: Control unit identifies ADD operation
Operand fetch: Read source registers R2 and R3 content
ALU operation: Control unit sets select lines for addition
ALU performs: Adds contents of R2 and R3
Result storage: Write result to destination register R1
Flag update: Update status flags based on result
Program counter: Increment to next instruction

questions.question: Explain the working of a ripple carry adder and its limitation. How does this affect ALU performance? Discuss alternative adder designs that overcome this limitation.
keyPoints: Ripple carry adder cascades full adders, each propagate carry to next
Sum of each stage depends on carry from previous stage
Propagation delay increases linearly with number of bits
For n-bit addition, delay is O(n) - becomes bottleneck
Carry lookahead adder computes carries in parallel
Uses generate and propagate signals to reduce delay to O(log n)
Parallel prefix adders further improve performance
